consensusSummary: The paper presents a novel approach to automated code smell detection using fine-tuned large language models, achieving strong performance metrics (92% precision, 87% recall) that outperform existing static analysis tools by 18%. Reviewers found the experimental setup thorough and the dataset construction well-documented. However, concerns were raised about evaluation gaps, particularly regarding language generalizability, coverage of newer ML-based comparison tools, and the need for practical validation with developers. A second round review indicates that additional experiments and a user study have addressed many initial concerns.
agreedStrengths: Impressive performance metrics with 92% precision and 87% recall compared to existing tools
Thorough and well-documented experimental setup and dataset construction
Strong comparison against existing static analysis tools like PMD and SonarQube
Compelling and solid approach to code smell detection using large language models
agreedConcerns: Limited language coverage in evaluation, with initial testing only on Java projects despite generalizability claims
Need for validation of practical utility and whether detected smells are actionable for developers
Incomplete comparison with newer ML-based code smell detection tools
Model performance concerns on less common smell types with limited training data
divergences: Reviewer 1 and 4 recommend acceptance while Reviewers 2 and 3 recommend minor revisions
Reviewer 3 emphasizes missing related work from ICSE 2025 while other reviewers do not mention literature gaps
Reviewer 4 notes that additional experiments on Python and JavaScript plus a user study have addressed concerns, while Reviewer 3's comments appear to predate these additions
